name: Thulium
name_origin: From Thule, an ancient name for a far-northern land, used by Cleve to denote his Scandinavian homeland.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Least abundant of the naturally occurring lanthanides apart from promethium (~0.5 ppm in crust); recovered chiefly as a minor component of xenotime and monazite.

compounds.name: Thulium(III) oxide
compounds.summary: Pale green solid used as a precursor for thulium-doped laser hosts and other thulium chemistry.

compounds.name: Thulium(III) chloride
compounds.summary: Bright yellow hygroscopic salt used as a precursor for thulium metal production.

compounds.name: Thulium(III) fluoride
compounds.summary: Used as feedstock for high-purity thulium metal and in fluoride glass for fibre amplifiers.

compounds.name: Thulium-doped yttrium aluminium garnet
compounds.summary: Active medium for ~2 micrometre solid-state lasers used in surgery and lidar.

uses: Active dopant in ~2 micrometre Tm:YAG and Tm:YLF lasers for surgery and remote sensing
Portable X-ray sources (Tm-170) for non-destructive testing in remote locations
Fibre-laser amplifiers operating in the 1.7-2.1 micrometre 'eye-safe' band
Blue-emitting phosphor activator in some euro banknote security inks
Dopant in solid-state and fibre lasers and high-temperature superconductors
reactivity_summary: Soft, silvery-grey ductile lanthanide; tarnishes slowly in dry air, faster in moist air. Reacts slowly with cold water and quickly with dilute acids. Two allotropes are known.
isotopes_summary: Thulium has only one stable, naturally occurring isotope (Tm-169). Many synthetic isotopes are known; Tm-171 (half-life 1.92 years) and Tm-170 (128.6 days, gamma emitter) are the most useful for medical and industrial applications.

citations.source: Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Thulium
IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements (2021)
CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd Edition
